goal: Order an espresso and a glass of water at the bar, pay at the cassa first.
failure_modes: Not paying at the cash register ('cassa') before ordering at the bar
Asking for a 'latte' and receiving just milk instead of 'caffè latte'
Not knowing to drink at the bar to avoid the table surcharge
pressure_elements: The barista works quickly and expects concise orders
The bar is crowded during morning rush
culture_notes: In many Italian bars you pay at the 'cassa' first and bring your receipt ('scontrino') to the barista
Cappuccino is traditionally a morning drink — ordering it after lunch may draw looks

goal: Order a primo and a secondo from the menu, ask for the house wine, and request the bill at the end.
failure_modes: Not understanding the traditional Italian course structure (primo, secondo, contorno)
Asking for parmesan on a seafood dish
Not knowing how to ask for the bill ('il conto, per favore')
pressure_elements: The waiter recites daily specials quickly
You need to understand unlisted specials described only verbally
culture_notes: Italian meals follow a course structure: antipasto, primo, secondo, contorno, dolce
Bread is often charged as 'coperto' (cover charge) — this is normal

goal: Check in, confirm your reservation for two nights, and ask about breakfast hours and nearby restaurants.
failure_modes: Mispronouncing your name so the receptionist cannot find the booking
Not understanding fast-spoken check-in details
Forgetting polite phrases like 'per favore' and 'grazie'
pressure_elements: The receptionist uses formal Italian and hotel-specific vocabulary
A group of guests arrives right after you
culture_notes: Italian hotels require your passport or ID at check-in by law
Breakfast ('colazione') is often included — ask 'La colazione è inclusa?'

goal: Ask for directions to the Colosseum and confirm whether it is within walking distance.
failure_modes: Not understanding directional words ('a destra', 'a sinistra', 'dritto')
Failing to politely get the person's attention first
Confusing landmarks or street names
pressure_elements: The passerby uses hand gestures extensively and speaks quickly
Busy street noise makes listening difficult
culture_notes: Start with 'Scusi' (formal) or 'Scusa' (informal) to get attention
Italians often give directions using landmarks rather than street names

goal: Ask to try on a jacket in a different size, inquire about available colors, and ask if there is a discount.
failure_modes: Not knowing Italian clothing sizes or how to ask for them
Struggling with color vocabulary beyond basic colors
Being too direct when asking about discounts — considered impolite
pressure_elements: The salesperson offers enthusiastic suggestions in rapid Italian
You need to understand descriptions of fabric and fit
culture_notes: Italian sizing differs from US/UK — ask 'Che taglia è questa?' to confirm
Sales ('saldi') happen in fixed seasons; asking for discounts outside sale periods is uncommon

goal: Explain that you have a stomach ache and ask for an over-the-counter remedy, confirming how often to take it.
failure_modes: Not knowing body-part vocabulary ('stomaco', 'mal di pancia')
Misunderstanding dosage instructions ('due volte al giorno')
Confusing 'farmacia' with 'parafarmacia' which has fewer products
pressure_elements: The pharmacist asks clarifying questions about your symptoms
Other customers are waiting in line behind you
culture_notes: Italian pharmacists are highly trained and can recommend treatments without a prescription for many ailments
Pharmacies rotate night and weekend duty — check the posted schedule on the door

goal: Buy a train ticket to Florence, ask about departure times, and confirm which platform to go to.
failure_modes: Confusing 'binario' (platform) numbers
Not understanding the difference between regionale and alta velocità trains
Failing to validate the ticket before boarding a regional train
pressure_elements: The ticket agent is handling a long queue and speaks quickly
Your train departs in 15 minutes
culture_notes: Regional train tickets in Italy must be validated ('convalidare') at the green machines before boarding
Trenitalia and Italo are two separate train operators — prices and platforms may differ

goal: Buy two museum tickets, ask about student discounts, and inquire if an audio guide is available in English.
failure_modes: Not knowing 'ridotto' (reduced price) or 'intero' (full price)
Forgetting to ask if a student ID is required for the discount
Not understanding the clerk's explanation of available tours
pressure_elements: A group tour is forming and the clerk is multitasking
You need to decide quickly before a time slot fills up
culture_notes: Many Italian museums offer free entry on the first Sunday of each month
EU citizens under 26 often get free or reduced entry — always ask

goal: Buy fresh tomatoes and mozzarella at a market stall, ask what is in season, and negotiate a small quantity.
failure_modes: Not knowing weight measurements ('un etto' = 100 grams, 'un chilo')
Touching the produce without asking — considered rude in many stalls
Not understanding rapid price quotes
pressure_elements: The vendor calls out to you enthusiastically and speaks fast
Other customers are reaching past you for items
culture_notes: At Italian markets, let the vendor handle the produce unless invited to choose yourself
Asking 'Cosa mi consiglia?' (What do you recommend?) is a great way to engage

goal: Politely explain you received the wrong dish, describe what you ordered, and confirm the replacement.
failure_modes: Being too aggressive or too passive when raising the issue
Not remembering the Italian name of the dish you ordered
Misunderstanding the waiter's proposed solution
pressure_elements: Your dining companions have already received their food
The restaurant is busy and the waiter is managing several tables
culture_notes: Italians value politeness in complaints — 'Mi scusi, credo ci sia un errore' works well
Sending food back is acceptable if done politely; the kitchen will correct it quickly

goal: Ask a local shopkeeper what time their store opens and closes, and whether they close for lunch.
failure_modes: Not understanding time expressions ('dalle nove alle diciotto')
Confusing 'aperto' (open) with 'chiuso' (closed)
Not greeting the shopkeeper before asking
pressure_elements: The shopkeeper answers while serving another customer
culture_notes: Many Italian shops close for a long lunch break ('pausa pranzo') from about 1 PM to 3:30 PM
Monday mornings and Sunday are common closing times for small shops

goal: Introduce yourself, say where you are from, explain you are visiting Italy, and ask the regular for a local tip.
failure_modes: Running out of things to say after the introduction
Using overly formal language in a casual café setting
Not understanding colloquial responses or Roman slang
pressure_elements: The regular is chatty and asks follow-up questions
Other patrons occasionally join the conversation
culture_notes: Italians love talking about food — asking 'Dove si mangia bene qui vicino?' is a great opener
A friendly 'Piacere!' (Nice to meet you!) sets a warm tone
